questions.to_find: Output printed by the program after stringcopy is called with overlapping pointers into array a

understand.plain: The array a holds the string "@#Hello World". stringcopy is called with destination a (a+0) and source a+2, which points to the character 'H'. The function copies characters one by one from a+2 ('H') onwards into a+0 onwards. Because the source pointer is always 2 positions ahead of the destination, the copy is safe and results in 'Hello World' at the start of a.

keywords.explain: Adding an integer n to a pointer advances it by n elements; a+2 points to the third character of array a (index 2).
Copying a string into itself when source offset > destination offset is safe because each source byte is read before its position is reached by the write pointer.
keywords.example: stringcopy(a, a+2) on "@#Hello" copies 'Hello' to the start of a.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='540' height='150' viewBox='0 0 540 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='10' y='22' class='lbl ink'>Before: a = "@#Hello World"</text><rect x='10' y='34' width='40' height='36' class='no'/><rect x='50' y='34' width='40' height='36' class='no'/><rect x='90' y='34' width='40' height='36' class='hi'/><rect x='130' y='34' width='40' height='36' class='hi'/><rect x='170' y='34' width='40' height='36' class='hi'/><rect x='210' y='34' width='40' height='36' class='hi'/><rect x='250' y='34' width='40' height='36' class='hi'/><text x='25' y='58' class='lbl ink'>@</text><text x='65' y='58' class='lbl ink'>#</text><text x='105' y='58' class='lbl ink'>H</text><text x='145' y='58' class='lbl ink'>e</text><text x='185' y='58' class='lbl ink'>l</text><text x='225' y='58' class='lbl ink'>l</text><text x='265' y='58' class='lbl ink'>o</text><text x='15' y='86' class='sub mut'>a[0]</text><text x='55' y='86' class='sub mut'>a[1]</text><text x='95' y='86' class='sub mut'>a[2]</text><text x='135' y='86' class='sub mut'>a[3]</text><text x='310' y='58' class='lbl ink'>...</text><text x='10' y='118' class='lbl ink'>After stringcopy(a, a+2): "Hello World"</text><text x='10' y='140' class='sub mut'>src(a+2='H') copied to dst(a+0); source stays 2 ahead</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Array a initially holds '@'(0), '#'(1), 'H'(2), 'e'(3), 'l'(4), 'l'(5), 'o'(6), ' '(7), 'W'(8)... stringcopy copies from index 2 ('H') into index 0, resulting in 'Hello World\0' at the start of a.

given.aim: Determine what printf prints after copying a+2 into a using stringcopy.

terms.meaning: Character array initialised with '@' at index 0, '#' at index 1, then 'Hello World' starting at index 2.
terms.connects: a+2 points to 'H', so stringcopy will copy 'Hello World' to the start of a.

terms.meaning: Destination pointer s starts at a[0], source pointer t starts at a[2]='H'. Copies until t reaches '\0', then null-terminates at s.
terms.example: Iteration 1: a[0]<-'H'; Iteration 2: a[1]<-'e'; ... Iteration 11: a[10]<-'d'; then a[11]<-'\0'.
terms.connects: Source is always 2 bytes ahead of destination, ensuring clean non-destructive copy.

terms.meaning: Prints the null-terminated string now stored at a, which is 'Hello World'.
terms.example: After the copy, a = "Hello World" followed by remaining old chars, but the null terminator at a[11] ends the string.

given.plan: Identify the character at index 2 of the initial string '@#Hello World' — it is 'H'. stringcopy then copies 'Hello World\0' into a[0]...a[11]. printf prints the string at a, which is now 'Hello World'.

steps.title: Map out the initial array contents
steps.note: The two-character prefix '@#' means a+2 lands on 'H', the start of 'Hello World'.

steps.title: Trace the stringcopy execution
steps.apply: s = a+0, t = a+2. Copy 11 characters: 'H','e','l','l','o',' ','W','o','r','l','d'. Then *s = '\0' at a[11]. a now contains 'Hello World\0'. Remaining bytes a[12]='d', a[13]='\0' are unreachable via the string.
steps.note: The source index is always 2 ahead of the destination index throughout the copy — no character is read after being overwritten.

steps.title: Determine the printed output
steps.apply: printf("%s\n", a) prints from a[0] until '\0' at a[11]. Output: 'Hello World'. This matches option B.
steps.note: Option A '@#Hello World!' would require the copy to fail; Option C 'ello World' would occur if a+2 were 'e'; Option D is just the original string without modification.

questions.formula_note: Pointer arithmetic on C arrays: a+n gives a pointer to the element at index n; stringcopy is a null-terminated string copy loop that safely handles source-ahead overlapping regions.

questions.answer: A. An array, each element of which is a pointer to a structure of type node
questions.to_find: What the declaration 'struct node *s[10]' defines s to be

understand.plain: The declaration 'struct node *s[10]' declares s as an array of 10 elements where each element is a pointer to struct node. The * binds to s (making each element a pointer), and [10] means there are 10 such pointers. This is different from a single pointer to an array of structs.

keywords.explain: A declaration Type *name[N] creates an array of N pointers, each pointing to Type.
A pointer to a struct holds the address of a struct variable; it does not contain the struct's fields itself.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='180' viewBox='0 0 420 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='10' y='22' class='lbl ink'>s[10]: array of 10 pointers</text><rect x='10' y='34' width='60' height='34' class='hi'/><rect x='70' y='34' width='60' height='34' class='bg'/><rect x='130' y='34' width='60' height='34' class='bg'/><text x='25' y='57' class='sub ink'>s[0] *</text><text x='85' y='57' class='sub ink'>s[1] *</text><text x='148' y='57' class='sub ink'>...</text><rect x='200' y='34' width='60' height='34' class='bg'/><text x='208' y='57' class='sub ink'>s[9] *</text><line x1='40' y1='68' x2='40' y2='110' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><rect x='10' y='110' width='100' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='20' y='132' class='sub ink'>struct node</text><text x='20' y='150' class='sub mut'>int i; float j;</text><text x='270' y='85' class='mut sub'>each element points to</text><text x='270' y='103' class='mut sub'>a struct node</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: s is an array of 10 boxes; each box holds a pointer (*) that points to a struct node containing fields int i and float j.

given.aim: Interpret the C declaration 'struct node *s[10]' correctly.

terms.term: struct node { int i; float j; };
terms.meaning: Defines a structure type called 'node' with two fields: an integer i and a float j.
terms.connects: This is the type that the pointers in s will point to.

terms.meaning: Declares s as an array of 10 elements; each element has type 'struct node *' (pointer to struct node).
terms.connects: The [] binds tighter than *, so [10] applies to s first — giving an array; then * gives the type of each element.

terms.meaning: In C, [] and () bind more tightly than *. So 'Type *name[N]' = array[N] of pointer-to-Type, NOT pointer-to-array[N]-of-Type.
terms.example: int *p[3] is array of 3 int-pointers; int (*p)[3] is pointer to array of 3 ints.
terms.connects: This rule determines option A is correct over option B.

given.plan: Apply C declaration precedence rules: [] binds before *, so s[10] is an array of 10, and each element is 'struct node *'. This matches option A.

steps.title: Apply C declaration parsing rules (right-left rule)
steps.apply: Starting at s: s is an array of 10 (because [10] is encountered first going right), then * going left means each element is a pointer, then 'struct node' is the pointed-to type. So s = array of 10 pointers to struct node.
steps.note: If parentheses were used as (*s)[10], it would be a pointer to an array of 10 struct nodes — a completely different type.

steps.title: Match to the given options
steps.apply: Option A: 'An array, each element of which is a pointer to a structure of type node' — this exactly matches our parsed meaning. Options B, C, D are all incorrect interpretations.
steps.note: Option D (array of structures, not pointers) would be declared as 'struct node s[10]' without the *.

solution.result: A. An array, each element of which is a pointer to a structure of type node

questions.formula_note: C declaration parsing right-left rule: postfix operators [] and () bind tighter than prefix *, so 'T *name[N]' declares an array of N pointers to T.

questions.to_find: Output of printf when struct fields are accessed via a cast char pointer with offsets

understand.plain: The struct Ournode has three consecutive char fields x, y, z. A pointer q points to the struct. By casting q to (char*), we can access individual bytes of the struct. (char*)q+1 points to the second byte (field y), and (char*)q+2 points to the third byte (field z). Dereferencing them gives y and z, which are '0' and 'a'+2='c' respectively. The %c format prints the characters themselves (not quoted), so output is '0, c'.

keywords.explain: For a struct with only char fields (no padding needed), the fields are laid out consecutively in memory: x at offset 0, y at offset 1, z at offset 2.
keywords.example: struct { char a, b; } s; (char*)&s+1 gives &s.b.

keywords.explain: Casting any pointer to char* allows byte-level access to the object's memory representation.
keywords.example: int n=0x41424344; *((char*)&n) gives the first byte (0x44 on little-endian).

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='160' viewBox='0 0 420 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='10' y='22' class='lbl ink'>Memory layout of struct Ournode p</text><rect x='10' y='34' width='100' height='50' class='bg'/><rect x='110' y='34' width='100' height='50' class='hi'/><rect x='210' y='34' width='100' height='50' class='hi'/><text x='40' y='55' class='lbl ink'>'1'</text><text x='140' y='55' class='lbl ink'>'0'</text><text x='240' y='55' class='lbl ink'>'c'</text><text x='30' y='74' class='sub mut'>x (offset 0)</text><text x='120' y='74' class='sub mut'>y (offset 1)</text><text x='220' y='74' class='sub mut'>z (offset 2)</text><text x='10' y='108' class='sub ink'>(char*)q   = &x  => '1'</text><text x='10' y='128' class='sub ok'>(char*)q+1 = &y  => '0'   printed</text><text x='10' y='148' class='sub ok'>(char*)q+2 = &z  => 'c'   printed</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Struct p occupies 3 consecutive bytes: byte 0 = '1' (x), byte 1 = '0' (y), byte 2 = 'c' (z, because 'a'+2='c'). (char*)q+1 gives '0' and (char*)q+2 gives 'c'.

given.aim: Determine what two characters are printed by accessing struct fields via a cast char pointer.

terms.connects: These are the values stored in the three consecutive bytes of the struct.

terms.meaning: Cast q (a struct pointer) to char* then add 1, yielding a pointer to the byte at offset 1 from the start of the struct — that is, field y.
terms.example: If q is at address 1000, (char*)q+1 is address 1001.

terms.meaning: Pointer to the byte at offset 2 from the start of the struct — that is, field z.
terms.example: If q is at address 1000, (char*)q+2 is address 1002.

given.plan: Since all fields are char (1 byte each, no padding between chars), the struct has no padding between x, y, z. Cast q to (char*) and use offset arithmetic to access y and z directly. Print them with %c.

steps.title: Compute the initialised field values
steps.apply: p.x = '1', p.y = '0', p.z = 'a'+2 = 'c'. The struct occupies 3 consecutive bytes in memory with no padding (all char fields).
steps.note: Char fields in a struct are never padded relative to each other — each is 1 byte.

steps.title: Evaluate the pointer expressions
steps.formula_raw: *((char*)q + n)  =>  byte at offset n from start of struct
steps.apply: *((char*)q+1): offset 1 from start of p = p.y = '0'. *((char*)q+2): offset 2 from start of p = p.z = 'c'.
steps.note: The %c format specifier prints the character itself, not its ASCII value or a quoted form.

steps.title: Determine the printed output
steps.apply: printf prints '0' then ', ' then 'c'. Output string: '0, c'. Matching option B: '0, c'.
steps.note: Option D uses single-quote notation '0', 'c' — that is not how printf %c works; it prints the bare character.

questions.formula_note: Struct memory layout for char-only fields has no padding; casting a struct pointer to char* enables byte-offset access to individual fields.
